mnemonics.title: BFS Queue Rhyme
mnemonics.content: BFS goes BROAD: Breadth uses Queue (First In, First Out) - like a line at a movie theater, level by level we go!

mnemonics.title: DFS Stack Rhyme
mnemonics.content: DFS goes DEEP: Depth uses Stack (Last In, First Out) - like a stack of plates, grab the top one first!

mnemonics.title: Hill Climbing Problems
mnemonics.content: Local Maxima Stuck: Hill Climbers often FALL - Flat plateau, ridge too tall - Local peak is not the greatest of all!

acronyms.usage: Explores level by level, uses Queue, finds shortest path in unweighted graphs
Explores depth first, uses Stack/recursion, memory efficient
Stores already visited nodes to avoid revisiting and cycles
Nodes to be explored, stored in appropriate data structure (Queue for BFS, Stack for DFS)
Queue property used by BFS - first node added is first to be explored
Stack property used by DFS - last node added is first to be explored

visualTips.visualization: Imagine water ripples spreading outward from a center point - each ripple represents a level being explored completely before going further out
Imagine a maze runner who follows one path as deep as possible before backtracking to try another path
Think of climbing a mountain in fog - you can only see nearby terrain and keep going 'up' until you reach a peak that turns out to be a local hill, not the highest mountain
Draw the tree horizontally with root at left: BFS expands row by row left to right; DFS goes down the leftmost branch completely before moving to next branch

analogies.analogy: Breadth-First Search is like checking every room on floor 1, then every room on floor 2, then floor 3 - you'll find the shortest route to the exit if floors represent distance

Depth-First Search is like a determined explorer who takes one hallway as deep as possible, marks dead ends, and backtracks to try another path - uses less 'bookmarks' (memory) than checking all rooms on each floor

Hill Climbing is like a hiker with a local compass who always walks in the direction of steepest ascent without considering the overall terrain - might reach a small hilltop and stop, missing the mountain peak
Local maximum is like being in a valley surrounded by small hills - going up from your current position leads to a peak, but it's not the highest peak in the entire mountain range
